translation: Alif, Lam, Ra. [This is] a Book which We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], that you might bring mankind out of darknesses into the light by permission of their Lord - to the path of the Exalted in Might, the Praiseworthy
commentary:
الٓر ۚ كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَيْكَ لِتُخْرِجَ ٱلنَّاسَ مِنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ

Alif Lam Ra. This is a magnificent book that We have sent down to you so that you may deliver people from darknesses into light. Before we examine this phrase about bringing people from darkness into light, we need to understand something important. The most famous instance of this expression appears in Surah Al-Baqarah, where Allah ﷻ says, "Allah is the guardian of those who believe. He brings them out from darknesses into the light." In that ayah, the subject performing this action is Allah ﷻ Himself. Here in Surah Ibrahim, the messenger is tasked with bringing people out of darkness into light.

Since the messenger is now doing this action, we need a crucial qualification so we never become confused that this is something the messenger accomplishes on his own. That's why the ayah continues: بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ (by the permission of their Master). Even though you are doing it, even though there is human effort involved, the divine will must be there. Human effort and divine will are combined in this beautiful phrasing.

إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ - to the path of Al-Aziz Al-Hamid, the ultimate authority, the One who is always to be praised, the One who deserves praise constantly.

translation: Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And woe to the disbelievers from a severe punishment
commentary:
ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَوَيْلٌ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مِنْ عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ

The ayah now clarifies who Al-Aziz Al-Hamid is. That's why Allah ﷻ is mentioned in the jarr form (genitive case) - the path of Allah ﷻ, to whom whatever is in the skies and whatever is in the earth belongs.

وَوَيْلٌ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مِنْ عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ - and the worst type of destruction, the worst kind of woe and terror is for the disbelievers because of an intense punishment.

translation: The ones who prefer the worldly life over the Hereafter and avert [people] from the way of Allah, seeking to make it (seem) deviant. Those are in extreme error
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَحِبُّونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا عَلَى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍۭ بَعِيدٍ

This ayah describes the disbelievers. Those who prefer worldly life over the next - disbelievers are given different qualifications and descriptions throughout the Quran, and each one must be examined carefully. In this particular case, the complaint of Allah ﷻ against disbelievers is that they give preference to this life over the next.

We are not disbelievers as Muslims, but if our life looks like we have priorities for this life and we have no preparation for the next life, then even though we're not kuffar, we're sharing some qualities with them. This isn't a healthy thing to do. This isn't something you can look at somebody walking down the street or a student next to you in class and judge. It doesn't work that way. This is a matter of our hearts, so everybody has to look deep inside their own conscience and ask themselves these questions as they read the ayat.

وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - and they obstruct themselves and others from Allah's path.

وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا - furthermore, they pursue crookedness in that path. Not only do they not want to follow it themselves because of their obsession with this life, but when they see somebody else following it, they put obstacles in their path. When that doesn't work, instead of trying to pull people back, they try to make deviations or criticisms about the religion itself. Whether that comes out in the form of videos that say "if you watch this, you won't be a Muslim anymore" or lists of alleged contradictions in the Quran - this is يَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍۭ بَعِيدٍ - they are in a far off misguidance. They're way out there.

translation: And We did not send any messenger except [speaking] in the language of his people to state clearly for them, and Allah sends astray [thereby] whom He wills and guides whom He wills. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسَانِ قَوْمِهِۦ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ ۖ فَيُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

We never sent any messenger at all except in the language of his people. This connects to what we discussed earlier about Surah Yusuf, Ar-Ra'd, Ibrahim, and Al-Hijr being a connected series of surahs whose themes are all tied to one another.

بِلِسَانِ قَوْمِهِۦ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ - in the language of his people so that he may clarify for them. Two things are being mentioned here: the messenger's tongue is Arabic, and the Quran has already been sent to him so he can deliver it to the people. So actually, between the lines, both Quran and Sunnah are being referenced - the lesson of the messenger when he uses his tongue (that's Sunnah) and the book that's been given to him (the Quran).

فَيُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَشَآءُ - and so Allah ﷻ misleads whoever He wants and whoever wants to be misled.

وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ - and He guides whoever He wants and whoever wants to be guided.

وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ - and He is the ultimate authority, the All-Wise.

translation: And We certainly sent Moses with Our signs, [saying], "Bring out your people from darknesses into the light and remind them of the days of Allah." Indeed in that are signs for everyone patient and grateful
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أَنْ أَخْرِجْ قَوْمَكَ مِنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ وَذَكِّرْهُم بِأَيَّىٰمِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ

We had no doubt already sent Musa (AS) with Our miraculous signs - that you should deliver your people from darknesses into light. We're seeing similar syntax to what appeared in Surah Ibrahim's beginning.

وَذَكِّرْهُم بِأَيَّىٰمِ ٱللَّهِ - and remind them of Allah's days. We're seeing flavors of earlier revelations here.

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ - in that there are multiple miraculous signs for everyone who will be repeatedly patient, continually patient, and extremely grateful.

These attributes at the end are no accident. People who are going to follow the legacies of those great messengers like Musa (AS) - that means they're going to be calling people from darknesses to light. They're going to be engaged in the activity of inviting people to Islam. If that's their activity, then they're obviously going to have to be very patient because they're going to face many challenges.

While they're facing these challenges, they should continually feel honored that Allah ﷻ chose them for these challenges as opposed to anybody else. The more challenges they face, not only are they able to cope with them because they're patient (صَبَّار), but they become increasingly grateful (شَكُور) for the opportunity to serve Allah ﷻ.

translation: And [recall, O Children of Israel], when Moses said to His people, "Remember the favor of Allah upon you when He saved you from the people of Pharaoh, who were afflicting you with the worst torment and were slaughtering your [newborn] sons and keeping your females alive. And in that was a great trial from your Lord
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ أَنجَىٰكُم مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ وَيُذَبِّحُونَ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَآءَكُمْ ۚ وَفِى ذَٰلِكُم بَلَآءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ

When Musa (AS) said to his nation: اذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ - make mention of Allah's favor upon you.

إِذْ أَنجَىٰكُم مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ - when He rescued you from the clutches, from the lineage of Fir'awn.

يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ - they were blackening your faces, darkening your faces, which means they were humiliating you, dealing you the worst forms of punishment.

وَيُذَبِّحُونَ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَآءَكُمْ - they were slaughtering your sons and allowing your women to live.

وَفِى ذَٰلِكُم بَلَآءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ - and in that there was a huge test, a trial from your Master, and it was great. It was no small test.

translation: And [remember] when your Lord proclaimed, 'If you are grateful, I will surely increase you [in favor]; but if you deny, indeed, My punishment is severe
commentary:
وَإِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكُمْ لَئِن شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ ۖ وَلَئِن كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذَابِى لَشَدِيدٌ

The previous ayah mentioned difficulty, and in times of difficulty you should think of sabr. The ayah even said لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ. But look at how the argument proceeds - the ayah goes immediately to gratitude (shukr).

You received the messenger. You have received guidance. When your master declared: لَئِن شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ - even if you are a little grateful, even if you are the least bit grateful. 

If Allah ﷻ had said وَلَئِن لَّا تَشْكُرُوا۟ ("and if you don't show gratitude"), that would be one thing. But He said لَئِن شَكَرْتُمْ - "even if" you were grateful, suggesting it's a highly unlikely thing. It's in the past tense, so it's one time. "If you were just to show gratitude one time..."

لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ - I will certainly, certainly, certainly - I swear to it - increase for you over and over and over again. Notice the emphasis with the lam (ل) to make it stronger, then the heavy noon with shaddah (نَّ) - the heaviest form of emphasis, doubled over.

وَلَئِن كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذَابِى لَشَدِيدٌ - and if you were to be ungrateful, even once. Now it's as though you don't even deserve to be mentioned. You see? لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ - "I will increase you" - كُمْ was referring to us. But when it comes to "I will take away from you, I will punish you," you don't even deserve to be mentioned. If you forgot Allah ﷻ, why should you be remembered?

إِنَّ عَذَابِى لَشَدِيدٌ - no doubt My punishment truly is intense.

translation: And Moses said, "If you should disbelieve, you and whoever is on the earth entirely - indeed, Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy
commentary:
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِن تَكْفُرُوٓا۟ أَنتُمْ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَغَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ

Musa (AS) said - notice the previous ayah was Allah's words. If you disbelieve, My punishment is intense. Now Musa (AS) is repeating Allah's words, resounding them to his nation.

إِن تَكْفُرُوٓا۟ أَنتُمْ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا - if you were to disbelieve, you and all of the people in the world, all together in the land.

فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَغَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ - certainly Allah ﷻ truly is in no need of you. And He's praised in and of Himself. He doesn't need your praise for His praise to exist.

translation: Has there not reached you the news of those before you - the people of Noah and 'Aad and Thamud and those after them? No one knows them but Allah. Their messengers brought them clear proofs, but they returned their hands to their mouths and said, "Indeed, we disbelieve in that with which you have been sent, and indeed we are, about that to which you invite us, in disquieting doubt
commentary:
أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعَادٍ وَثَمُودَ ۛ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۛ لَا يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۚ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَرَدُّوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَهُمْ فِىٓ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كَفَرْنَا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ وَإِنَّا لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَنَآ إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ

أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ - didn't the news of those who came much before you ever come to you? The nations of Nuh (AS), 'Ad, Thamud.

وَٱلَّذِينَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ - and those who came after them, much after them.

لَا يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ - no one knows them except Allah ﷻ. Allah ﷻ buried those nations so deep in the ground you can't even find their ruins. Nobody knows where they are.

جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ - their messengers came to them with clear proofs.

فَرَدُّوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَهُمْ فِىٓ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ - then they put their hands back in their mouths. This is actually an expression of frustration in old Arabic. Nowadays, the expression of frustration is often filthy language, but this phrase means they were so frustrated.

وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كَفَرْنَا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ - they openly said, "We disbelieve, no doubt about it. We're the ones that disbelieve in whatever you've been sent with."

وَإِنَّا لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَنَآ إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ - and we're the ones that, especially in whatever you're calling us to, we are in a doubt that keeps growing and puts more and more suspicion. We're very skeptical about your agenda.

In the previous surah, we ended with "we're not sure about you being a messenger." That argument is being furthered here too.

translation: Their messengers said, "Can there be doubt about Allah, Creator of the heavens and earth? He invites you that He may forgive you of your sins, and He delays your death for a specified term." They said, "You are not but men like us who wish to avert us from what our fathers were worshipping. So bring us a clear authority
commentary:
۞ قَالَتْ رُسُلُهُمْ أَفِى ٱللَّهِ شَكٌّ فَاطِرِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكُم مِّن ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرَكُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا تُرِيدُونَ أَن تَصُدُّونَا عَمَّا كَانَ يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَا فَأْتُونَا بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ

Their messengers said: أَفِى ٱللَّهِ شَكٌّ - is there any doubt in Allah ﷻ?

فَاطِرِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ - the Creator of the skies and the earth?

Interestingly, in the previous ayah when they said "we have doubt," they didn't say "we have doubt in Allah ﷻ." What did they say? "We have doubt in you as a messenger, we have doubt in your message, whatever you're calling us to."

Instead of saying "no, no, I really am a messenger," the messengers go back to the heart of the matter: Are you denying Allah ﷻ? Because doubting us means you're doubting our message, which has nothing to do with anything but Allah ﷻ Himself.

يَدْعُوكُمْ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكُم مِّن ذُنُوبِكُمْ - He's inviting you, not us. You think we're inviting you? Actually, it's Allah ﷻ inviting you so He can forgive you because of your sins.

وَيُؤَخِّرَكُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى - and He keeps giving you extra time. He's giving you delayed time until a known deadline, a well-named, well-proclaimed deadline.

قَالُوٓا۟ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا - then they came to their next argument. This is what we learned before about those who disbelieve. Now we're seeing what's going on inside their minds. "You're nothing like skin and flesh like us. What makes you so special?"

تُرِيدُونَ أَن تَصُدُّونَا عَمَّا كَانَ يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَا - "You just want to turn us away from what our fathers used to worship. You want us to turn our backs to our country, our nation, our history, our civilization, our tradition. Is that what you want?"

فَأْتُونَا بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ - "Bring forward any clear proof."

When people like this say something like this, the nature of their arguments being irrational is already embedded in the argument. If this had been said at the beginning - "bring us some clear proof, we'll consider it" - that would mean they're interested in hearing what you have to say, they're interested in hearing your argument. They didn't say that.

First they said, "You want us to turn away from our legacy, our history, our tradition, our fathers, our ancestors. My grandfather did this. His grandfather did this. My great-great-great-grandfather built this temple. We've been here for generations." In other words, they've already given you reasons for which they will never leave. Then on top of that, they're saying "bring us any proofs" - because even if you bring them proof, they'll still say "but my father, but my grandfather."

The first layer of their reasoning isn't rational, it's not based on evidence - it's just tradition. Then on top of that, they say "what proof do you have anyway?" You will notice this line of reasoning repeat itself in conversations across the world, within Muslims and outside of Muslims.

You'll talk to some members of your family about something being wrong. They'll say "no, that's how we do things in our family." Thirty minutes later, they'll say "well, what's the ayah or hadith anyway?" Even if you bring the ayah or hadith or whatever, they're going to say "but that's how we do things in our family. We don't need this from you."

The first line of reasoning is loyalty to tradition. Then you superficially just say "oh, you don't have any proof anyway."

translation: Their messengers said to them, "We are only men like you, but Allah confers favor upon whom He wills of His servants. It has never been for us to bring you evidence except by permission of Allah. And upon Allah let the believers rely
commentary:
قَالَتْ لَهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ إِن نَّحْنُ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَمُنُّ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَمَا كَانَ لَنَآ أَن نَّأْتِيَكُم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ

Their messengers said to them: إِن نَّحْنُ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ - we are nothing but mortal beings just like yourselves. We never said anything different. We'll agree with you there.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَمُنُّ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ - however, Allah ﷻ chooses to favor, He favors whomever He wants from out of His slaves. In other words, the favor of becoming a messenger.

وَمَا كَانَ لَنَآ أَن نَّأْتِيَكُم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ - and it's not becoming of us, it's not our place to bring you any kind of authoritative proof except by Allah's permission.

وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ - and upon Allah ﷻ, believers are supposed to rely.

translation: And why should we not rely upon Allah while He has guided us to our [good] ways. And we will surely be patient against whatever harm you should cause us. And upon Allah let those who would rely [indeed] rely
commentary:
وَمَا لَنَآ أَلَّا نَتَوَكَّلَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ وَقَدْ هَدَىٰنَا سُبُلَنَا ۚ وَلَنَصْبِرَنَّ عَلَىٰ مَآ ءَاذَيْتُمُونَا ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ

وَمَا لَنَآ أَلَّا نَتَوَكَّلَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ - what would be wrong with us that we wouldn't rely entirely upon Allah ﷻ?

وَقَدْ هَدَىٰنَا سُبُلَنَا - and He has guided us to His multiple pathways.

وَلَنَصْبِرَنَّ عَلَىٰ مَآ ءَاذَيْتُمُونَا - and we will absolutely remain patient with our utmost commitment over whatever pains you cause us, whatever torture you dish our way. We're going to remain patient.

وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ - and upon Allah ﷻ, those who place trust should continue to place their trust. In other words, if anybody's ever going to place trust, it only should be in Allah ﷻ. Everything else will disappoint.

translation: And those who disbelieved said to their messengers, "We will surely drive you out of our land, or you must return to our religion." So their Lord inspired to them, "We will surely destroy the wrongdoers
commentary:
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِرُسُلِهِمْ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِنَآ أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِى مِلَّتِنَا ۖ فَأَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ لَنُهْلِكَنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

Those who disbelieved said to their messengers: لَنُخْرِجَنَّكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِنَآ - we will expel you, we will get you out of here. There's a difference between أَرْض (land) and saying مِّنْ أَرْضِنَآ - "this is our land. You don't get to bring your message here. That doesn't belong here. This is how we do in our country. We have to get you people out of here and your message out of here."

أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِى مِلَّتِنَا - the only alternative: we will make sure you are part of the melting pot, make sure you assimilate. We will return you into our loyal nation. We will make you one with us. In other words, whatever makes you different as Muslims, we have to make sure that's all gone so you're just like everybody else. You need to blend in, you need to merge and fuse in with what the culture is.

At this point, it's important to understand Islam's stance on culture. Many fired-up youth get all excited and say "Islam versus culture," but it's not that simple. Islam came to an Arab culture. You can never remove culture from people - ever. How they dress, their customs in how they deal with people, how they hold their wedding celebrations - these are parts of different regions of the world that have different customs and traditions, and there's nothing wrong with them.

Islam came with a bunch of ethics and teachings and dos and don'ts. Practically speaking, so long as you observe those, you don't have to eliminate a culture to have Islam. You can't say "I just follow Islam, I don't follow any culture." Yes, you do. Everyone has a culture, whether it's American or modern or Asian or African. Everybody has a culture - even down to the food we eat and the kinds of things we do in our free time. That's also part of our culture.

Islam didn't come to eliminate those things. Islamic history is full of such a diverse ummah - European Muslims, African Muslims, Asian Muslims, with several language traditions within the Asian Muslims. All of them retained their culture. What did Islam come to do? To clean up all these cultures and bring them the purity of Islam, to get rid of the filth from those cultures.

These people, however, don't want to accept Islam in any way, shape or form. Anything of Islam must be against our culture, must not stand for us.

Since we're on the subject, an important observation: there are two things that empower people, that drive people's actions from a sociological point of view. There's education or knowledge, and then there's culture. People do things because they learned about something and they know how to do it a certain way. Also, people do things because of their cultural backgrounds.

Which of those two things do you think is a more powerful force in society? Culture, by far. Even if you know about something to be wrong, but everybody in your culture does it, guess what? You're going to get swept away with the tide and you're going to do it. Culture means basically what is considered normal in your society - whatever is normal in your society is your culture. People are driving within a certain speed limit - that's the culture. People are speaking in the English language - that's a culture. Certain behaviors are considered normal - that's part of our culture.

What happens with Islam is it sometimes counters the dominant culture, whether it's in our dress code or our mannerisms or how we conduct ourselves. Corporate culture sometimes doesn't align well with Islam - you can't have those office parties, you can't mingle at the bar where people go after the project is finished, you can't be doused in alcohol after you win the game. Sports culture can differ with Islam.

When you have to start drawing those lines, people start looking at you as weird, like you don't belong with us, you're kind of strange. "We need to make you normal like us."

فَأَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ لَنُهْلِكَنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - their Master revealed to them: "We will destroy the wrongdoers." These people that are issuing this threat to the messengers - We will annihilate them Ourselves.

translation: And We will surely cause you to dwell in the land after them. That is for he who fears My position and fears My threat
commentary:
وَلَنُسْكِنَنَّكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَنْ خَافَ مَقَامِى وَخَافَ وَعِيدِ

وَلَنُسْكِنَنَّكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ - and We will station you in the land even after them. We will give you stability in the land.

ذَٰلِكَ لِمَنْ خَافَ مَقَامِى - that is for the one who feared standing in front of Me.

وَخَافَ وَعِيدِ - and was afraid of My threatening promise. وَعِيد is actually a scary promise, a threatening promise. The word وَعِيد is never used in a positive sense. That's why it's خَافَ وَعِيدِ - be afraid of My promise, My threat basically.

translation: And they requested victory from Allah, and disappointed, [therefore], was every obstinate tyrant
commentary:
وَٱسْتَفْتَحُوا۟ وَخَابَ كُلُّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ

These prophets, messengers and their followers asked Allah ﷻ for victory. The response came: every tyrant was completely destroyed. The word جَبَّار comes from the root meaning to force a broken bone straight. A Jabbar is the kind of ruler who declares, "This is what I think straight behavior is, and I will make you do it whether you like it or not." This is the essence of tyranny—forcing one's will upon others.

The description عَنِيد adds another dimension. While we often translate this as "stubborn," the classical meaning is more specific: someone who stubbornly refuses to do what is right. It's not just stubbornness, but stubbornness in opposition to truth and goodness. These tyrants, with all their power and obstinacy, were utterly defeated. The word خَابَ captures this total failure—they bit the dust, they were completely annihilated.

translation: Before him is Hell, and he will be given a drink of purulent water
commentary:
مِّن وَرَآئِهِۦ جَهَنَّمُ وَيُسْقَىٰ مِن مَّآءٍ صَدِيدٍ

Beyond this worldly destruction awaits Jahannam. There they will be given صَدِيد to drink. This word refers to the fluid that oozes from a fresh wound—a mixture of blood, water and pus with a strange, horrifying texture. This is what they will be forced to consume.

translation: He will gulp it but will hardly [be able to] swallow it. And death will come to him from everywhere, but he is not to die. And before him is a massive punishment
commentary:
يَتَجَرَّعُهُۥ وَلَا يَكَادُ يُسِيغُهُۥ وَيَأْتِيهِ ٱلْمَوْتُ مِن كُلِّ مَكَانٍ وَمَا هُوَ بِمَيِّتٍ ۖ وَمِن وَرَآئِهِۦ عَذَابٌ غَلِيظٌ

The verb يَتَجَرَّعُ describes trying to drink something gulp by gulp because it's so difficult to consume—like forcing down bitter medicine. The person must drink it bit by bit, struggling with each gulp. Yet even this proves nearly impossible, as يُسِيغُ tells us—it barely goes down the throat smoothly. He almost keeps throwing it up, but it's being forced down anyway.

Death comes at him from every direction, and he desperately wishes to die, to escape this torment. Yet death is not permitted—وَمَا هُوَ بِمَيِّتٍ—he is not allowed to die. And the ayah concludes with a chilling statement: beyond all of this lies غَلِيظ, intense and merciless punishment. Everything described so far—the destruction, Jahannam, the vile drink that won't go down, the wish for death that won't come—this isn't even the real punishment yet. That's just the beginning. The actual ruthless punishment starts after all of this.

translation: The example of those who disbelieve in their Lord is [that] their deeds are like ashes which the wind blows forcefully on a stormy day; they are unable [to keep] from what they earned a [single] thing. That is what is extreme error
commentary:
مَّثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ ۖ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ كَرَمَادٍ ٱشْتَدَّتْ بِهِ ٱلرِّيحُ فِى يَوْمٍ عَاصِفٍ ۖ لَّا يَقْدِرُونَ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلضَّلَـٰلُ ٱلْبَعِيدُ

Allah ﷻ now provides an example of those who rejected their Master. Their deeds are like رَمَاد—ash or fine dust. This is significant because disbelievers do perform good deeds. Atheists engage in charity, they establish hospitals, they help others. On the Day of Judgment, they will bring whatever they've accomplished: "I donated, I started a children's hospital, I helped this many people." All of this is acknowledged—yes, you did these things. But when they try to present these deeds, they're like someone trying to hold dust in their hands on an intensely windy day.

The ayah emphasizes this wind in two ways: اشْتَدَّتْ بِهِ الرِّيحُ—the wind was intensified, it became severe. And this happens فِى يَوْمٍ عَاصِفٍ—on a day that is itself mercilessly stormy, a day when the wind doesn't let up at all. If it were just a gentle breeze, you might be able to save some of the dust. But this is relentless, all day long. What will they be able to show? لَّا يَقْدِرُونَ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ—they will have no control over what they earned, not in any capacity. It flies away completely.

This is الضَّلَـٰلُ الْبَعِيدُ—the ultimate, distant misguidance.

translation: Have you not seen that Allah created the heavens and the earth in truth? If He wills, He can do away with you and produce a new creation
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ إِن يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ

Haven't you seen that Allah ﷻ created the heavens and the earth بِٱلْحَقِّ—with purpose, with truth? This connects directly to the previous imagery. Many humanitarians and ethical people say, "I found my calling—I want to help children, I want to save endangered species, I want to preserve the environment." These are noble causes, but they miss the first and most fundamental purpose: to recognize who created all of this in the first place.

If you truly want to preserve creation, you must first understand that Allah ﷻ, if He wanted, could remove all of you—every species, every human—and bring forth an entirely new creation. You're concerned about preserving life? Then begin by respecting and acknowledging the One who originated all life. Start there, and then, yes, be a humanitarian, be an environmentalist. These are all good causes, but they must be built on the right foundation.

translation: And that is not difficult for Allah
commentary:
وَمَا ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ بِعَزِيزٍ

And this—replacing all of creation—is not عَزِيز upon Allah ﷻ, not difficult or hard to accomplish. The word عَزِيز can mean something rare or difficult to achieve. For Allah ﷻ, recreating all of existence is not challenging in the least.

translation: And they will come out [for judgement] before Allah all together, and the weak will say to those who were arrogant, "Indeed, we were your followers, so can you avail us anything against the punishment of Allah?" They will say, "If Allah had guided us, we would have guided you. It is all the same for us whether we show intolerance or are patient: there is for us no place of escape
commentary:
وَبَرَزُوا۟ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا فَقَالَ ٱلضُّعَفَـٰٓؤُا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا لَكُمْ تَبَعًا فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّغْنُونَ عَنَّا مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ قَالُوا۟ لَوْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ لَهَدَيْنَـٰكُمْ ۖ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْنَآ أَجَزِعْنَآ أَمْ صَبَرْنَا مَا لَنَا مِن مَّحِيصٍ

They will be brought face to face with Allah ﷻ altogether. The weak—the followers—will say to those who saw themselves as great, those who were arrogant: "We used to follow you. Are you able to help us now from Allah's punishment in any way?"

Picture the scene: the followers of false leaders, the fans who attended every concert, who followed every word of their idols, now stand before the Fire. Their celebrities, their leaders, their influencers are right in front of them. The followers call out: "Can you help us a little bit over here?"

The leaders' response is stunning in its arrogance. Even now, standing before punishment, they don't accept blame: لَوْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ لَهَدَيْنَـٰكُمْ—"Had Allah guided us, we would have guided you." They still deflect responsibility.

Then they add: سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْنَآ—it's the same for us—أَجَزِعْنَآ أَمْ صَبَرْنَا—whether we lose our patience and break down, or whether we maintain composure. The word جَزِعَ is the opposite of صَبَرَ. When someone is overwhelmed and can't control themselves, that's jazaʿa. When they hold on and maintain perseverance, that's sabara. The leaders are saying: whether we panic or stay calm, مَا لَنَا مِن مَّحِيصٍ—we have no place to hide, no escape from this trouble.

The word مَحِيص refers to a place where you hide to get away from danger—like during war, when people are shooting and you find some small hole to take shelter. There's no such refuge from Allah's judgment.

translation: And Satan will say when the matter has been concluded, "Indeed, Allah had promised you the promise of truth. And I promised you, but I betrayed you. But I had no authority over you except that I invited you, and you responded to me. So do not blame me; but blame yourselves. I cannot be called to your aid, nor can you be called to my aid. Indeed, I deny your association of me [with Allah] before. Indeed, for the wrongdoers is a painful punishment
commentary:
وَقَالَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لَمَّا قُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَعَدَكُمْ وَعْدَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَوَعَدتُّكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُكُمْ ۖ وَمَا كَانَ لِىَ عَلَيْكُم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍ إِلَّآ أَن دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَٱسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِى ۖ فَلَا تَلُومُونِى وَلُومُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُم ۖ مَّآ أَنَا۠ بِمُصْرِخِكُمْ وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُصْرِخِىَّ ۖ إِنِّى كَفَرْتُ بِمَآ أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِن قَبْلُ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

Now the leader of all these leaders speaks—Shaytan himself. When the matter has been completely decided, when everything is finished, he addresses his followers.

"Allah ﷻ promised you, and His promise was وَعْدَ ٱلْحَقِّ—absolutely true, without any doubt. And I promised you too, فَأَخْلَفْتُكُمْ—but I went back on my promise. I broke it." Then comes his defense: وَمَا كَانَ لِىَ عَلَيْكُم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍ—"I didn't have any authority over you, any control. إِلَّآ أَن دَعَوْتُكُمْ—All I did was invite you. I sent you the flyers, I sent you the invitation, I kept whispering in your ear. فَٱسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِى—But you responded to me. I didn't force anything. I'm just the sales guy; you signed the papers."

فَلَا تَلُومُونِى وَلُومُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُم—"Don't blame me. Blame yourselves." Then he adds something almost darkly comical in its finality: مَّآ أَنَا۠ بِمُصْرِخِكُمْ—"I'm not going to be screaming to help you." What would he even yell about? What help could possibly come? وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُصْرِخِىَّ—"And you're not going to be doing that for me either, because what's the point?"

إِنِّى كَفَرْتُ بِمَآ أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِن قَبْلُ—"I reject all the shirk you committed with me from long before. I deny it. I have nothing to do with it. That was all you."

The ayah closes with the inevitable: إِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ—those who did wrong will have painful, extreme punishment.

translation: And those who believed and did righteous deeds will be admitted to gardens beneath which rivers flow, abiding eternally therein by permission of their Lord; and their greeting therein will be, "Peace
commentary:
وَأُدْخِلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ ۖ تَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيهَا سَلَـٰمٌ

After this scene of rejection and blame, the contrast: those who believed and did righteous deeds. Notice the verb used—أُدْخِلَ, the passive form. They "are entered" into Paradise, meaning they are escorted, guided in. It's not that they're walking themselves; rather: "Please, this way. Don't look over there—that's too intense for you. Here's your gate. Here's your floor."

They enter gardens beneath which rivers flow, خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا—remaining there permanently—بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ—by the permission of their Master.

تَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيهَا سَلَـٰمٌ—Their greeting there is continuously Salam. Not just one "salam" at the gate, but constant greetings of peace, life and rejuvenation. "Salam! Oh my goodness, you made it! Where do you live? Above that mountain? I'm near your mountain! Let me just move my mountain over—it's all good!" This incredible company where everyone is greeting each other endlessly. "What century are you from? You're from the time of Prophet Musa (AS)? What did the water look like when it parted? Show me on your screen!"

The atmosphere is one of continuous joy, reunion and peace.

translation: Have you not considered how Allah presents an example, [making] a good word like a good tree, whose root is firmly fixed and its branches [high] in the sky
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ ضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُهَا ثَابِتٌ وَفَرْعُهَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ

Haven't you seen how Allah ﷻ presents an example? A كَلِمَة طَيِّبَة—a good word, a pure word—is like a طَيِّبَة شَجَرَة—a good, pure tree. This good word is lā ilāha illAllah, Muhammad Rasulullah ﷺ—the declaration of faith, the testimony of Iman.

أَصْلُهَا ثَابِتٌ—Its roots are firmly planted, deeply established in the ground. This represents our Iman, which lives inside our hearts. Just as the roots of a strong tree go deep underground where you cannot see them, our Iman is internal, deeply rooted within.

وَفَرْعُهَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ—And its branches extend toward the sky. This represents our actions, our deeds. Just as our hands reach upward toward the sky in prayer, our deeds are many and they reach high. They're not low-level, earthbound actions—they're elevated, reaching toward acceptance from the heavens.

This is the beautiful relationship: deeply rooted Iman produces high-reaching deeds. The visible branches and fruits come from invisible roots. That's the example of a good word—internal belief manifesting in external righteous action.

translation: It produces its fruit all the time, by permission of its Lord. And Allah presents examples for the people that perhaps they will be reminded
commentary:
تُؤْتِىٓ أُكُلَهَا كُلَّ حِينٍۭ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهَا ۗ وَيَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ

تُؤْتِىٓ أُكُلَهَا كُلَّ حِينٍ—It gives its produce constantly, all the time. In Surah Al-Baqarah, we read about a garden whose fruit is constant and whose shade is constant. Here, Allah ﷻ says the fruits—the good outcomes—are continuous. This refers to the good deeds that stem from Iman. The good deeds of this world transform into the rewards of Jannah. The believer's tree produces fruit constantly, بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهَا—by permission of its Master.

Think about what a fruit-bearing tree provides: people can come and take shade under it, they can benefit from the fruit, and inside every fruit are seeds to start the process over again. Iman becomes contagious. One good tree leads to another, until you have a forest of Iman.

The tree analogy is beautiful for another reason: trees take a long time to grow. Human development is like this. You cannot expect people to transform into Sahabah overnight. Even the Sahabah themselves didn't become who they were instantly—it took time. When Allah ﷻ talks about the development of the Companions, He compares them to a farm that matures gradually over the course of a year, requiring constant care from the farmer.

Developing people, building character—this takes time, both for yourself and for others. It requires constant work, like a gardener who is always attentive to his plants. And notice: even when a tree becomes mature and healthy and is giving fruit to others, does it no longer need water? Of course it still needs water. Even if you're a source of benefit to others, you never stop needing nourishment yourself. And what is the nourishment? Revelation from the sky—Wahy.

Even the teacher, even the scholar—everyone constantly needs to return to that source.

وَيَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ—Allah ﷻ gives these examples to people so they can make an effort to remember, to reflect and take lessons.

translation: And the example of a bad word is like a bad tree, uprooted from the surface of the earth, not having any stability
commentary:
وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ ٱجْتُثَّتْ مِن فَوْقِ ٱلْأَرْضِ مَا لَهَا مِن قَرَارٍ

Now the opposite example: a كَلِمَة خَبِيثَة—a filthy, corrupt word. This is the word of shirk, of disbelief, of false ideologies like extreme nationalism or pure materialism. It's like a خَبِيثَة شَجَرَة—a vile tree, meaning something more like a thorny shrub.

ٱجْتُثَّتْ مِن فَوْقِ ٱلْأَرْضِ—It has been uprooted from above the earth. It doesn't have deep roots going into the ground; it's just sitting on top of the surface. Think of an ugly plant covered in thorns with no greenery, no beauty, just sitting there on the earth. You could literally walk up to it, pick it up and throw it away.

مَا لَهَا مِن قَرَارٍ—It has no station, no stability, no firm place. When the wind blows, it gets blown away with it.

The contrast is striking: one tree has roots so deep you cannot see them and branches reaching to the sky, while the other just sits on the surface with no foundation at all. Why has this worthless plant been around? Simply because no one has come to clean up the garden yet. That's the only reason. Since false beliefs have no deeply rooted foundation, they will eventually just be picked up and tossed away.

This continues the imagery from earlier in the surah, where we saw how easily the oppressor was removed from the earth. Just like that, a plant without roots can be picked up and blown away by the wind.

translation: Allah keeps firm those who believe, with the firm word, in worldly life and in the Hereafter. And Allah sends astray the wrongdoers. And Allah does what He wills
commentary:
يُثَبِّتُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱلْقَوْلِ ٱلثَّابِتِ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَيُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ ۚ وَيَفْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ مَا يَشَآءُ

يُثَبِّتُ ٱللَّهُ—Allah ﷻ keeps firm—ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟—those who truly believe—بِٱلْقَوْلِ ٱلثَّابِتِ—with the firm word, the established statement. This is the deeply rooted Iman, the firm conviction.

فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ—In worldly life and in the next. Allah ﷻ provides stability and firmness in both worlds for those whose Iman is rooted deep.

وَيُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ—And Allah ﷻ will let those who did wrong go astray. They have no roots, no foundation, so they get lost.

وَيَفْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ مَا يَشَآءُ—And Allah ﷻ does whatever He wills.

translation: Have you not considered those who exchanged the favor of Allah for disbelief and settled their people [in] the home of ruin
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ كُفْرًا وَأَحَلُّوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ دَارَ ٱلْبَوَارِ

Haven't you looked toward those who replaced the favor of Allah ﷻ with disbelief? They were given Makkah, the blessed city. They had the Kaaba among them. They possessed the legacy of Prophets Ibrahim (AS) and Ismail (AS). They still maintained traditions of Hajj and the ritual slaughter, all learned from this prophetic heritage. They took all of this blessing, all of this favor, and turned it into kufr.

وَأَحَلُّوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ دَارَ ٱلْبَوَارِ—And they caused their nation to settle in the home of destruction. They brought destruction upon themselves.

translation: [It is] Hell, which they will [enter to] burn, and wretched is the settlement
commentary:
جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَهَا ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْقَرَارُ

جَهَنَّمَ—Jahannam—يَصْلَوْنَهَا—they will be cast into it, they will roast in it. وَبِئْسَ ٱلْقَرَارُ—And what a horrible place to remain.

Remember how in ayah 26, the evil tree مَا لَهَا مِن قَرَارٍ—doesn't have a place to stay, no stability, no station? Well, now it does have a place where it gets deeply established, and that place is Jahannam.

translation: And they have attributed to Allah equals to mislead [people] from His way. Say, "Enjoy yourselves, for indeed, your destination is the Fire
commentary:
وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا لِّيُضِلُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۗ قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا۟ فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ

وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا—They placed competitors with Allah ﷻ, they set up rivals and equals to Him. لِّيُضِلُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ—So they could misguide people away from His path.

قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا۟—Tell them: Enjoy yourselves, go ahead and indulge. فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ—Your final destination, your ultimate return, is headed toward the Fire. Not just "in the Fire eventually," but you're making progress toward it right now, constantly moving in that direction.

translation: [O Muhammad], tell My servants who have believed to establish prayer and spend from what We have provided them, secretly and publicly, before a Day comes in which there will be no exchange, nor any friendships
commentary:
قُل لِّعِبَادِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ لَّا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَلَا خِلَـٰلٌ

قُل لِّعِبَادِىَ—Say to My slaves—ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟—those who have come to believe. Allah ﷻ now gives direct instruction to the believers: don't become like those people who were given the favor of Allah ﷻ and squandered it, who replaced it with kufr.

يُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ—Establish the prayer. Make sure you never abandon your connection with Allah ﷻ.

وَيُنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ—And spend from whatever We have provided them—سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً—secretly and openly. Give in ways that are seen and in ways that are hidden.

مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ—Before a Day arrives—لَّا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ—in which there is no transaction, no buying or selling—وَلَا خِلَـٰلٌ—nor is there any khilal.

خِلَـٰل refers to long-term, deep friendship, as opposed to temporary companionship. On that Day, neither business dealings nor friendships will be of any benefit.

translation: It is Allah who created the heavens and the earth and sent down rain from the sky and produced thereby some fruits as provision for you and subjected for you the ships to sail through the sea by His command and subjected for you the rivers
commentary:
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَأَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجَ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ رِزْقًا لَّكُمْ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلْفُلْكَ لِتَجْرِىَ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِۦ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرَ

Allah ﷻ is the One who created the heavens and the earth. He sent water down from the sky, and through it He brought forth all kinds of fruits as provision for you.

وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلْفُلْكَ—He subdued for you, He put to work for you, ships—لِتَجْرِىَ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِۦ—so they may sail in the ocean by His command.

وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرَ—And He subdued for you all the rivers.

The verb سَخَّرَ is powerful. We use it when we tame animals so we can use them for our benefit. Allah ﷻ describes oceans, rivers and ships as being tamed by Him so we can use them. Just as we harness animals, Allah ﷻ has harnessed these vast forces of nature and placed them at our service.

This final passage reminds us of the countless blessings surrounding us—water from the sky, fruits from the earth, ships that sail, rivers that flow. All of this is preparation for what comes next in the surah, where Allah ﷻ will continue enumerating His favors upon humanity, demanding that we recognize them and respond with gratitude rather than replacing His blessings with disbelief.

translation: And He subjected for you the sun and the moon, continuous [in orbit], and subjected for you the night and the day
commentary:
وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ دَآئِبَيْنِ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ

Allah ﷻ continues His enumeration of the blessings He has bestowed upon humanity, specifically highlighting the celestial bodies that govern our understanding of time. The word سَخَّرَ means "He subdued" or "He put to work for your sake." This is a powerful concept—Allah ﷻ has placed the sun and the moon in service to humanity.

Think of the sun and moon as the natural clock and calendar that Allah ﷻ has hung in the sky for us. The sun marks our days, and the moon tracks our months. These aren't arbitrary creations—they serve specific functions in organizing human life and worship.

The word دَآئِبَيْنِ comes from the root د-أ-ب, which describes someone who works tirelessly even when exhausted. The mufassirun explain that the sun and moon are perpetually in motion, never taking a break despite their constant labor. They are exhausted, yet they continue their courses without rest. One day, Allah ﷻ will finally say, "Take a break," and that will be when one crashes into the other at the end of time. But until that moment arrives, they remain at work.

The ayah then adds: وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ—"And He subdued for you the night and the day." This is the natural continuation of the discussion about the sun and moon, as these celestial bodies create the alternation of night and day that structures our lives.

But there's a deeper implication here. When something is put to work for you, it suggests you have more important things to do. Consider how this works in worldly hierarchies: everyone works for the king. Someone cleans his quarters, someone prepares his meals, someone handles his correspondence. Why? Because the king has more critical responsibilities than everyone else. He has a support staff so he can focus on matters of state rather than menial tasks.

Now apply this principle to what Allah ﷻ is telling us. If He has subdued the ships, the rivers, the sun, the moon, the night, and the day all for our service, what does that tell us? It means what we have to do must be genuinely important. Allah ﷻ has made the entire natural world our secretary, our support system. This should make us realize that our primary job—worshiping and serving Allah ﷻ—is so crucial that He has arranged the cosmos to facilitate it.

But here's the tragedy: sometimes when a king's son—the prince of the next generation—sees that everything is taken care of for him, he doesn't grasp the weight of his responsibilities. Instead, he lives a life of luxury, does no meaningful work, and ends up destroying the empire. This is precisely what happens to human beings across generations.

The first generations who received Allah's message understood clearly: the world has been placed at our service so we can serve Allah ﷻ. We have the honored job of being His servants. But the next generation thinks, "The world is at our service—we can just relax. Everything's working for us." This is the spiritual decay that sets in.

According to Allah ﷻ, all these facilities we enjoy in life aren't meant to put us in a state of comfortable relaxation. They're supposed to create a sense of urgency, making us realize: my work is so important that Allah ﷻ subdued the sun and the moon for my service. So what service do I need to provide in return?

translation: And He gave you from all you asked of Him. And if you should count the favor of Allah, you could not enumerate them. Indeed, mankind is [generally] most unjust and ungrateful
commentary:
وَءَاتَىٰكُم مِّن كُلِّ مَا سَأَلْتُمُوهُ ۚ وَإِن تَعُدُّوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ لَا تُحْصُوهَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ

Allah ﷻ declares: "And He has granted you from everything that you asked for." The word سَأَلْتُمُوهُ is important to understand properly in Arabic. The root س-أ-ل can mean "to ask," but it also carries the meaning "to demand." There's a significant difference between these two concepts.

Let me illustrate: I might ask you for a glass of water with my tongue—that's an explicit request. But even if I don't verbally ask, my throat is demanding water. There's a su'al that comes from the tongue, and there's also a su'al that comes from the body's needs—from the lungs, from the throat, from every cell.

So when Allah ﷻ says He gives you from everything you ask for, there are two levels of meaning. First, there are explicit requests—people who consciously call upon Allah ﷻ, and He responds to them. But second, there are the implicit demands of our very existence. We have needs like food, air, shelter, and safety. Allah ﷻ provides these even without us articulating them. Our physical existence itself is asking.

This connects beautifully to what we read in Surah Ar-Rahman: يَسْـَٔلُهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ—"Everyone in the heavens and earth asks Him" (55:29). You might object: "But the carpet isn't asking Allah ﷻ. The atheist isn't asking Allah ﷻ. The Hindu isn't asking Allah ﷻ." What does that ayah mean then?

Yes, the believers are consciously asking Allah ﷻ. But the verse encompasses everyone in the heavens and earth in the meaning of placing demands. Even the disbeliever's lungs are asking for oxygen. His heart is asking for its next contraction and expansion. Every system in his body is requesting sustenance, and Allah ﷻ is responding to those requests constantly, whether that person acknowledges Him or not.

Allah ﷻ then says: وَإِن تَعُدُّوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ لَا تُحْصُوهَآ—"And if you were to try to count the blessings of Allah, you would not be able to encompass them." Notice the singular form نِعْمَتَ —it's not the plural نِعَم. This is profound. Allah ﷻ is saying that if you tried to count even one blessing, you wouldn't be able to fully encompass it, to circle around it completely. Even if you could enumerate aspects of it with your tongue, you wouldn't realize how deep and multifaceted that single blessing truly is.

Let me give you an example. I often discuss with my wife, who studied speech and communications and worked in speech therapy, the extraordinary complexity of human communication. Just the fact that I'm speaking to you right now involves countless miracles. Thoughts form in my mind and translate into coordinated movements of my tongue, lips, and vocal cords. Those movements create sound waves that travel through air and strike your eardrums. Your brain then processes these vibrations into meaningful words that you comprehend and remember. All of this happens in split seconds—I'm not pausing after every word to let you process it, render it, save it to your mental hard drive, back it up, and then load the next audio file.

Anyone familiar with computing can appreciate how much processing this requires. It's not merely recording data—it's simultaneous processing, interpretation, and storage. And you're supposed to be able to recall this information at a moment's notice. Sometimes there's a lag—like when a large file is opening and you see the hourglass spinning. This does happen occasionally in the middle of a lecture when you can't quite retrieve a word. But most of the time, speech just flows effortlessly.

What an immense blessing speech is. What an incredible favor is the ability to understand language. And here's another layer: throughout each day, you and I experience many thoughts. We communicate much of our inner world to others: "Let's go somewhere." "I'm hungry." "I didn't like what you said." "I loved that." And so on. Now imagine not having the ability to communicate your thoughts. Some people suffer from disorders where they have thoughts but cannot find words to express them.

Consider young children who have tantrums—what parents call "the terrible twos" (though it actually extends through ages two, three, and four). Why are the twos especially challenging? Because the child is just beginning to acquire language and put sentences together, but not enough to communicate everything happening in his mind. This frustrates him deeply. He gets angry because he cannot express what he wants to say. That's where the rage comes from. Language, then, is actually a tremendous relief from human stress. The ability to talk alleviates so much tension.

Why do you think therapists primarily want you to talk? When you visit one, they say, "Just tell me what's going on." You might resist: "I don't want to talk about it." But they insist: "No, no, just speak." After two hours of talking, you often say, "Doctor, I actually feel better. I don't know why I'm paying you—I did all the work!"

This is just one blessing—the gift of speech. Allah ﷻ is saying that if you tried to fully encompass even this single blessing, you couldn't do it. Yet what does the human being do?

إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ—"Indeed, the human being is extremely wrongdoing, excessively ungrateful." Both ظَلُومٌ and كَفَّارٌ are in the intensive forms—fa'ul and fa'al respectively. These forms don't just indicate excess; they indicate repetition and persistence.

A khabbaz bakes bread over and over. A bayya' sells repeatedly. A jazzar chops meat again and again. Similarly, a kaffar engages in disbelief and ingratitude over and over and over. This means the human being keeps stumbling upon opportunities to reflect and be grateful, and he keeps ignoring them, again and again. He's not just a kafir (one-time disbeliever); he is a kaffar—someone who persistently, habitually covers over the truth and refuses to be grateful despite repeated reminders.

This sets up a powerful contrast with what comes next: the attitude of Ibrahim (AS).

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], when Abraham said, "My Lord, make this city [Makkah] secure and keep me and my sons away from worshipping idols
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ رَبِّ ٱجْعَلْ هَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدَ ءَامِنًا وَٱجْنُبْنِى وَبَنِىَّ أَن نَّعْبُدَ ٱلْأَصْنَامَ

We now encounter the opposite attitude—that of a truly grateful servant. Ibrahim (AS) says: رَبِّ ٱجْعَلْ هَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدَ ءَامِنًا—"My Master, make this land secure." This is significant because this is after the city has been built. He has returned years later, and what was once barren desert is now a flourishing, populated city. His supplication has evolved.

The first time we encountered this dua was in Surah Al-Baqarah, where Ibrahim (AS) said: رَبِّ ٱجْعَلْ هَـٰذَا بَلَدًا ءَامِنًا—"My Lord, make this a secure city" (2:126). At that time, he asked for food and provision because all he could see was desert. He prayed: "Provide its people with fruits," and asked that they be believers.

Now when he returns, he sees everything has been taken care of—water supply, fruit, food, all the physical necessities. So he's not going to ask for those material things anymore. He's going to ask for something more important. And if you examine the progression of his supplications, you'll appreciate the wisdom of Ibrahim (AS).

Previously in that conversation in Al-Baqarah, there was a dialogue between Ibrahim (AS) and Allah ﷻ about who would receive these blessings. Allah ﷻ clarified that the disbelievers would enjoy temporary provision but face consequences. At the end of that exchange, Ibrahim (AS) requested: "From my descendants, make an ummah that submits to You, and send among them a messenger."

Here, he goes straight to the heart of the matter, acknowledging that earlier conversation. He modifies his supplication and says: "Make this city peaceful"—because just because it's flourishing doesn't mean it will remain safe. That's never guaranteed.

Then he makes a deeply personal and humble request: وَٱجْنُبْنِى وَبَنِىَّ أَن نَّعْبُدَ ٱلْأَصْنَامَ—"And keep me and my sons away from worshiping idols."

The word جَنَّبَ comes from جَنْب, which means "side." The verb جَنَبَ means to sidestep something, to avoid something, to keep at a distance. When you make something mutajannib, you prevent someone from falling into something. It's as if Ibrahim (AS) is asking: "Allah, make sure that You cause me to avoid this. Make certain that You sidestep me away from this danger. Keep me at a safe distance."

Why is he so concerned about أَصْنَام? In Arabic and in the Quran, there are multiple words for idols—sanam, wathn, taghut, and others. Each has its nuance. Asnam specifically refers to idols made of gold, silver, or expensive wood that are carved for commercial purposes. They're exotic, transportable merchandise that gets manufactured, shipped, and sold. The Arabs even had a term for it: صِنَاعَةُ الْأَصْنَامِ —"the idol-making industry."

Ibrahim (AS) uses this specific word because he has firsthand experience with it. His father Azar wasn't just a casual idol worshiper—he was in the idol business. Ibrahim (AS) grew up in a home where idols were manufactured and sold. He witnessed this corruption from the inside. He saw the idols carved in his own household, then distributed and worshiped by masses of people. This is why his hatred for idol worship is so intense, and why he doesn't consider it a minor threat.

This personal history explains the depth of his concern and why he makes this supplication with such urgency, even for himself and his righteous descendants.

translation: My Lord, indeed they have led astray many among the people. So whoever follows me - then he is of me; and whoever disobeys me - indeed, You are [yet] Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۖ فَمَن تَبِعَنِى فَإِنَّهُۥ مِنِّى ۖ وَمَنْ عَصَانِى فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Ibrahim (AS) explains his fear: رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ—"My Master, indeed they have led many people astray." He's not speaking theoretically. He has personally witnessed idols mislead countless souls. He's seen the spiritual devastation they cause.

Notice that he doesn't just say أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيرًا —"they have misled many." He says إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيرًا. The particle إِنَّ is used for emphasis, but here it also conveys that this is a weighty, serious matter. This isn't a minor issue—it's a grave threat that has destroyed nations.

Then Ibrahim (AS) shifts from speaking about his children to a broader principle: فَمَن تَبِعَنِى فَإِنَّهُۥ مِنِّى—"So whoever follows me, then he is from me." He doesn't want to restrict the conversation only to his biological descendants. He's saying: "Whoever follows my path, whoever adheres to this monotheistic message, that person counts as my people. He's on my side. He belongs to my spiritual family."

وَمَنْ عَصَانِى—"And whoever disobeys me." Now notice something remarkable: he doesn't complete this sentence in the expected way. You would anticipate him saying: "Then he is not from me" or "Then punish him" or "Then turn away from him." But Ibrahim (AS) doesn't say any of that. Instead, he says something that's always true, something that cannot be refused:

فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ—"Then certainly You are Ever-Forgiving, Especially Merciful."

This is the spiritual intelligence of a prophet. If he had explicitly said, "Forgive them, O Allah," the response might have been, "No." But instead, he states a truth about Allah's nature—"You are Ever-Forgiving, Ever-Merciful"—and lets that truth speak for itself. It's an indirect plea that's more powerful precisely because it's indirect. He's learned to modify his words with wisdom and adab.

translation: Our Lord, I have settled some of my descendants in an uncultivated valley near Your sacred House, our Lord, that they may establish prayer. So make hearts among the people incline toward them and provide for them from the fruits that they might be grateful
commentary:
رَّبَّنَآ إِنِّىٓ أَسْكَنتُ مِن ذُرِّيَّتِى بِوَادٍ غَيْرِ ذِى زَرْعٍ عِندَ بَيْتِكَ ٱلْمُحَرَّمِ رَبَّنَا لِيُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ فَٱجْعَلْ أَفْـِٔدَةً مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ تَهْوِىٓ إِلَيْهِمْ وَٱرْزُقْهُم مِّنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْكُرُونَ

Notice how Ibrahim (AS) now shifts from رَبِّ ("My Master") to رَبَّنَا ("Our Master"). This change in address indicates that his children and family are now included in the supplication.

إِنِّىٓ أَسْكَنتُ مِن ذُرِّيَّتِى بِوَادٍ غَيْرِ ذِى زَرْعٍ—"Indeed I have settled some of my descendants in a valley without cultivation." He's reminding Allah ﷻ of what he did in complete trust. "I took my family—my wife and infant son—and placed them in the middle of certain death: a barren, waterless desert. I did this purely out of trust in You."

عِندَ بَيْتِكَ ٱلْمُحَرَّمِ—"Near Your Sacred House." This phrase is historically significant. When Ibrahim (AS) left his family in that desolate place, was the House of Allah ﷻ physically there? No, it wasn't built yet. But he acknowledges that this was always the location of Your Sacred House. This is a historical clue that the foundations of the Ka'bah were originally established by Adam (AS), and those foundations remained. That's why later we find the ayah: وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ ٱلْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ ٱلْبَيْتِ—"And when Ibrahim was raising the foundations of the House" (2:127). He didn't build it from scratch—he raised existing foundations.

رَبَّنَا لِيُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ—"Our Lord, so that they may establish prayer." Ibrahim (AS) states his purpose clearly: "I left them there for this reason—so that they would establish the worship of You, so that this place would become a center of devotion to You alone."

فَٱجْعَلْ أَفْـِٔدَةً مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ تَهْوِىٓ إِلَيْهِمْ—"Then make the hearts of people incline toward them." These are extraordinarily beautiful words. أَفْـِٔدَةً is the plural of فُؤَاد, which refers to the passionate, emotional core of the heart—not just the physical heart, but the seat of deep feelings and attachments.

Ibrahim (AS) is saying: "Make that passionate part of people's hearts do something. What should happen to it?" The verb is تَهْوِىٓ, which comes from هَوَى. This word literally means "to desire," but it carries connotations of intense, consuming desire.

In the Quran, you often see the plural أَهْوَاء translated as "desires": لَا تَتَّبِعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ—"Do not follow their desires." The word هَوَى describes a desire so powerful that you forget who you are, you forget your place in society, you forget your role and responsibilities. Usually it's used in a negative context—someone who's obsessed with something to the point of losing themselves.

But here, Ibrahim (AS) is asking for people to have this level of passion for the Sacred House. They should love it so intensely that they forget everything else—their status, their titles, their wealth, their positions in society.

This is remarkably beautiful because when people come to the Ka'bah for Hajj, they come from what purpose? To fulfill the pilgrimage. And when they perform Hajj, what happens? They forget their thrones, their designer suits, their prestigious titles, their degrees. Everyone becomes exactly the same—two simple white cloths. They come with that overwhelming love that makes them forget everything else.

Another dimension of هَوَى is that it also means "to fall." The Arabs say: هَوَى النَّجْمُ "The star fell." So تَهْوِىٓ can also mean "the hearts of people should fall toward them." What's the modern English expression? "Falling in love." You literally can't stand anymore—your heart just falls. This is what people's hearts should do when it comes to this Sacred House and its people.

وَٱرْزُقْهُم مِّنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ—"And provide them with fruits." Previously in Surah Al-Baqarah, when Ibrahim (AS) first made this supplication, he talked about provision before he talked about faith. But now he restructures his dua. He says: "Put real love of this House in their hearts, and then provide them all kinds of fruits."

لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْكُرُونَ—"So that they may be grateful." Even the food isn't the ultimate goal. The goal is that they can do more shukr (gratitude) to Allah ﷻ.

Why is this structure important? Why does this story appear in this particular place in the surah? Look at what came right before: إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ—"Indeed, the human being is extremely wrongdoing, excessively ungrateful." 

Ibrahim (AS) is the perfect contrast. He's saying: "Every time they eat fruit, let it make them more grateful to You." This is how the human being is supposed to be—not a kaffar (persistently ungrateful), but constantly turning blessings into occasions for gratitude.

translation: Our Lord, indeed You know what we conceal and what we declare, and nothing is hidden from Allah on the earth or in the heaven
commentary:
رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ تَعْلَمُ مَا نُخْفِى وَمَا نُعْلِنُ ۗ وَمَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ

رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ تَعْلَمُ مَا نُخْفِى وَمَا نُعْلِنُ—"Our Master, indeed You know what we conceal and what we declare openly." Ibrahim (AS) acknowledges that Allah ﷻ has complete knowledge of both the hidden and the manifest—the private thoughts in the heart and the public declarations of the tongue.

This is particularly meaningful in the context of Hajj, which was just mentioned. During Hajj, everyone is making loud proclamations—اللَّهُمَّ لَبَّيْكَ, all the supplications and dhikr happen out loud. But what's really happening inside each person's heart? That's something entirely different. Some hearts are completely present and sincere; others are distracted, going through motions. Allah ﷻ knows both the external ritual and the internal reality.

وَمَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ—"And nothing whatsoever remains hidden from Allah—not anything in the earth nor in the sky." This is a complete statement of Allah's absolute knowledge. Nothing escapes His awareness—not the smallest particle on earth, not the farthest star in the heavens.

Ibrahim (AS) makes this declaration of Allah's comprehensive knowledge as a foundation for what comes next. He's establishing: "You know everything about our situation, our needs, our struggles, our sincerity—both what we can express and what remains locked in our hearts."

translation: Praise to Allah, who has granted to me in old age Ishmael and Isaac. Indeed, my Lord is the Hearer of supplication
commentary:
ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى وَهَبَ لِى عَلَى ٱلْكِبَرِ إِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى لَسَمِيعُ ٱلدُّعَآءِ

ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى وَهَبَ لِى عَلَى ٱلْكِبَرِ إِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ—"All praise and gratitude belongs to Allah who granted me, despite old age, Isma'il and Ishaq." After all the supplications, Ibrahim (AS) now shifts to expressing gratitude for answered prayers. He was given children when such a blessing seemed impossible—in his advanced age when most men would have given up hope.

Notice the order: he mentions Isma'il (AS) first, then Ishaq (AS). This is chronologically accurate—Isma'il (AS) was the older son. This detail is significant because the biblical narrative mentions "sacrifice your only son," but then erroneously identifies that son as Ishaq (Isaac). However, when scholars examine the evidence honestly, they recognize that if it was the "only son," it could only have been Isma'il (AS), who was born first and was indeed Ibrahim's only son for years before Ishaq (AS) was born.

The only way certain biblical interpreters circumvented this clear evidence was by claiming that Isma'il (AS) was "illegitimate"—a claim that Muslims categorically reject. The Quranic order here subtly affirms the truth: Isma'il (AS) was the firstborn, blessed son of Ibrahim (AS).

إِنَّ رَبِّى لَسَمِيعُ ٱلدُّعَآءِ—"Indeed, my Master is the Hearer of supplication." Ibrahim (AS) concludes by affirming a truth he has experienced personally. Allah ﷻ is السَّمِيعُ—not just "hearing" in the sense of sound waves hitting an eardrum, but truly listening, understanding, and responding. He hears the dua—the calling out, the plea, the prayer—and He answers according to His wisdom.

This entire passage beautifully demonstrates the contrast between two types of human beings: the ungrateful one described earlier who ignores countless blessings and repeatedly covers the truth, and the grateful servant exemplified by Ibrahim (AS) who turns every blessing into an opportunity for praise, who worries about spiritual dangers even for his righteous descendants, and who structures his entire life around gratitude and worship of Allah ﷻ alone.

translation: My Lord, make me an establisher of prayer, and [many] from my descendants. Our Lord, and accept my supplication
commentary:
رَبِّ ٱجْعَلْنِى مُقِيمَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِى ۚ رَبَّنَا وَتَقَبَّلْ دُعَآءِ

"My Master, make me an establisher of prayer, and from my offspring as well. Our Master, accept my supplication."

This beautiful dua from Prophet Ibrahim (AS) contains a subtle but profound transition. He begins by saying "my Master" (رَبِّ), making a personal request: make me someone constant in establishing prayer. Then he extends this concern to his children, saying "and from my offspring" (وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِى).

The moment he mentions his children, something remarkable happens in the text. The address shifts from رَبِّ (my Master) to رَبَّنَا (our Master). This isn't merely a grammatical shift. Ibrahim (AS), upon mentioning his children, immediately brought them into the dua with him. He didn't just pray for them while they were absent. He gathered them and they made dua collectively. This is why the text shifts to "our Master" - Ismail (AS) and his family joined in.

This teaches us something essential about parenting in Islam. When you're worried about your children's spiritual state, don't just make dua alone in your room. Bring them together and make dua with them. Get them involved. Let them hear your concerns elevated to Allah ﷻ and let them join their voices with yours.

The phrase وَتَقَبَّلْ دُعَآءِ (and accept my supplication) is particularly striking. Of course, when anyone makes dua, they assume it should be accepted. But this specific phrasing carries an air of desperation. It's as if Ibrahim (AS) is saying, "Whatever else You may or may not accept, please, please accept this one." This is the dua of a father deeply concerned about the spiritual trajectory of his family.

translation: Our Lord, forgive me and my parents and the believers the Day the account is established
commentary:
رَبَّنَا ٱغْفِرْ لِى وَلِوَٰلِدَىَّ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلْحِسَابُ

"Our Master, forgive me and my parents and the believers on the Day when the reckoning will stand."

Ibrahim (AS) continues his supplication, asking for forgiveness for himself, his parents, and all believers on the Day of Judgment. The phrase يَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلْحِسَابُ is significant - when the accounting "stands" or is "established." The word يَقُومُ suggests something coming into existence, standing upright, becoming a reality.

translation: And never think that Allah is unaware of what the wrongdoers do. He only delays them for a Day when eyes will stare [in horror]
commentary:
وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَـٰفِلًا عَمَّا يَعْمَلُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ ۚ إِنَّمَا يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ لِيَوْمٍ تَشْخَصُ فِيهِ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ

"And never think that Allah ﷻ is unaware of what the wrongdoers do. He only delays them for a Day when eyes will stare in horror."

Here begins what appears to be a completely different subject - the punishment awaiting the disbelievers. But why does this passage appear immediately after Ibrahim's (AS) beautiful duas for his family and descendants?

The context is crucial. The Quraysh, the very people torturing the Muslims in Makkah, claimed to be descendants of Ibrahim (AS). They might have heard these ayahs and assumed they were automatically qualified to be recipients of Ibrahim's (AS) supplications simply by lineage. Allah ﷻ immediately clarifies: being a biological descendant means nothing without faith and righteous action. These beautiful duas do not apply to the Quraysh oppressors.

Simultaneously, this passage serves another purpose for the Muslims suffering persecution. When someone tells you to be patient under oppression, a natural human response is: "I'm being patient, but what about them? Are You going to do something about them?" It's like when one child wrongs another and the parent says, "Be patient." The wronged child responds, "I know, but are you at least going to do something to them?"

Allah ﷻ gives the Muslims this consolation. Don't ever think He is غَافِلًا (unaware, heedless) about what these wrongdoers are doing. The word choice is powerful: وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ - "never, ever think." This is emphatic. He sees everything. He knows everything.

إِنَّمَا يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ - He is only delaying them. The delay isn't forgetfulness or weakness. It's strategic. لِيَوْمٍ تَشْخَصُ فِيهِ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ - for a Day when eyes will be petrified, frozen in horror. The word تَشْخَصُ describes eyes that cannot move, cannot blink, locked in a state of absolute terror.

translation: Racing ahead, their heads raised up, their glance does not come back to them, and their hearts are void
commentary:
مُهْطِعِينَ مُقْنِعِى رُءُوسِهِمْ لَا يَرْتَدُّ إِلَيْهِمْ طَرْفُهُمْ ۖ وَأَفْـِٔدَتُهُمْ هَوَآءٌ

"Racing ahead, their heads raised up, their glance not returning to them, and their hearts empty air."

The description intensifies. The previous ayah said their eyes would be frozen. Now we see them مُهْطِعِينَ - rushing forward with their eyes locked on something they cannot look away from. This word describes someone running toward a target without ever taking their eyes off it, like a sprinter who never breaks eye contact with the finish line.

مُقْنِعِى رُءُوسِهِمْ - with their heads held back. The image is of someone whose head is forced upward, like a horse whose reins are pulled back, forcing its neck up. These criminals on Judgment Day cannot blink, their eyes are staring upward at the sky, and they're running in this contorted position.

لَا يَرْتَدُّ إِلَيْهِمْ طَرْفُهُمْ - their glance does not return to them. They cannot even look at themselves. Their gaze is completely locked.

وَأَفْـِٔدَتُهُمْ هَوَآء - and their hearts are empty air. The word هَوَآءٌ literally means a moving breeze. Their hearts have become like fluttering air - empty, terrified, unable to settle. This is the state of absolute psychological collapse.

translation: And, [O Muhammad], warn the people of a Day when the punishment will come to them and those who did wrong will say, "Our Lord, delay us for a short term; we will answer Your call and follow the messengers." [But it will be said], "Had you not sworn, before, that for you there would be no cessation
commentary:
وَأَنذِرِ ٱلنَّاسَ يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ فَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ أَخِّرْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ نُّجِبْ دَعْوَتَكَ وَنَتَّبِعِ ٱلرُّسُلَ ۗ أَوَلَمْ تَكُونُوٓا۟ أَقْسَمْتُم مِّن قَبْلُ مَا لَكُم مِّن زَوَالٍ

"And warn the people of the Day when punishment will come to them, and those who did wrong will say, 'Our Master, delay us for a brief term; we will answer Your call and follow the messengers.' Did you not swear before that you would never face decline?"

On that Day, the wrongdoers cry out: رَبَّنَآ أَخِّرْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ - "Our Master, give us just a little more time, a nearby deadline." They're not asking for years, just a brief extension. "Give us a couple of weeks and we'll fix everything."

نُّجِبْ دَعْوَتَكَ وَنَتَّبِعِ ٱلرُّسُلَ - "We will respond to Your call and follow the messengers." Notice the intelligence mixed with delusion. They know that repentance doesn't require much time. They know that once you accept Islam and submit, all previous sins are wiped clean. They know they could fix their situation quickly if given another chance.

The response is devastating: أَوَلَمْ تَكُونُوٓا۟ أَقْسَمْتُم مِّن قَبْلُ مَا لَكُم مِّن زَوَالٍ - "Weren't you the ones who swore before that you would never face any decline?" 

زَوَالٍ refers to the declining of the sun from its zenith. When the sun is at its peak and begins to descend, that's zawal. But these people said they wouldn't even experience that much of a decline. They swore their nation would always be on top, that they would never lose their status, that they were invincible. What happened to those oaths now?

translation: And you lived among the dwellings of those who wronged themselves, and it had become clear to you how We dealt with them. And We presented for you [many] examples
commentary:
